TEimEZVjN9o.title: SpaceX: The Most Tragic IPO In Stock Market History

buy_recommendations.reason: The creator is buying VGT instead of SpaceX stock and selling shares of the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ). VGT is Vanguard's information technology ETF which consistently outperforms the triple Qs, has lower management fees, and tracks an index that will not include SpaceX. This allows exposure to technology sector growth without the valuation risks and governance concerns of SpaceX.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: SpaceX is priced at 94 times revenue, not earnings, which is almost 50% more expensive than Palantir (the highest price-to-sales ratio in the S&P 500 at 65x), while growing almost two times slower and with much lower margins. The company is not profitable. The valuation grew 12 times faster than revenue growth. 93% of SpaceX's total addressable market is AI (primarily the troubled xAI acquisition), where the company has minimal traction and must compete against entrenched players like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. The core space business (Falcon 9, Starship) is operating at a $657 million loss despite being 24 years old. Starship development lacks regulatory approval, passenger transport regulations, and has no timeline for commercial viability. The company also has severe governance issues: Elon Musk controls 85% of votes with only 42% of shares, has been granted $175 billion in performance-based shares that he can vote and use as collateral before vesting with no requirement to actually build a Mars colony, and has a corporate opportunity carve-out allowing him to take business opportunities elsewhere. Shareholders waive jury trial rights and have no real governance power.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: Significant downside risk given the 94x sales valuation, unprofitability, slowing revenue growth relative to valuation expansion, governance control concentrated in a single individual with conflicting incentives, and forced inclusion in the Nasdaq 100 index at only 4-5% free float, which will artificially inflate the stock price as passive investors are forced to buy.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: The creator is selling shares of the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) because SpaceX will be added to this index just 15 trading days after its IPO under Nasdaq's new fast entry rule. This forces massive passive index funds to buy SpaceX stock at an inflated price despite the company's questionable fundamentals and governance. The stock has an extremely low free float of only 4-5%, meaning insiders controlling 95%+ of shares can effectively manipulate the price. Insiders can sell into a market where index funds are forced to buy, creating an artificial price inflation that will eventually unwind.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: The forced buying from index funds will artificially inflate SpaceX's stock price in the short term, creating valuation bubbles within QQQ that will eventually correct downward when the speculative demand subsides and reality of SpaceX's fundamentals becomes apparent.

key_warnings: Nasdaq index rule changes have eliminated protective mechanisms for retail investors. The elimination of the 10% minimum free float requirement and introduction of a 'fast entry' rule allowing companies into the Nasdaq 100 just 15 trading days after IPO removes critical safeguards that previously protected investors from overpriced IPOs and price manipulation by insiders with low free float.

SpaceX IPO represents a massive wealth transfer from retail investors to insiders. The combination of extreme overvaluation (94x sales), concentrated insider voting control (Elon Musk controls 85% of votes), modified lockup periods allowing insiders to sell 20% of eligible shares immediately after first earnings report, and forced buying pressure from index funds creates conditions for insider enrichment at the expense of public shareholders.

xAI/Grok acquisition is a liability masquerading as a growth asset. xAI represents 93% of SpaceX's total addressable market but has minimal enterprise traction (2 out of 1,000 users), faced a major scandal producing 4+ million explicit deepfake images including minors in an 11-day period, is facing multiple regulatory investigations and lawsuits, and Elon Musk himself stated the AI needs to be rebuilt from scratch. All 11 co-founders except Musk have left the company.

SpaceX's core space business is financially unviable at current operating metrics. The rocket business (Falcon 9, Starship) generated $4.1 billion in revenue but spent $4.8 billion operating it, resulting in a $657 million operating loss despite being 24 years old. SpaceX spends 60% more capital expenditure on xAI data centers than the entire space business combined, and Starship accounts for only 14% of total CapEx despite being critical to the company's valuation narrative.

CLLNEr3d8CM.title: Top Stocks I'm Buying For Huge Growth In June 2026

buy_recommendations.reason: Qualcomm just struck a deal to supply custom AI data center chips to ByteDance, gaining access to the largest AI market on Earth where Nvidia is locked out due to export controls. The custom ASIC chips are not on restricted export controls lists, making them production-ready through 2026 and 2027. Qualcomm becomes embedded in ByteDance's chip design and production process, making them harder to replace over time. Beyond ByteDance, Qualcomm's Orion CPU directly competes with Nvidia's Vera chip, and their AI 200 and AI 250 inference accelerators enter the market this year and next year. The stock has doubled over the last two months as the market reprices Qualcomm from a smartphone chip company to a competitor on two major fronts: data centers and edge computing. Qualcomm's automotive segment brought in $1.3 billion in revenues last quarter with 38% year-over-year growth, outpacing Nvidia's 6% automotive growth.

Arm just announced their AGI CPU, the first production chip Arm has ever designed, manufactured, and branded for themselves. This is a tactical entry into the data center CPU market after 35 years of collecting royalties from both sides of the chip war. The AGI CPU is more powerful than Nvidia's Vera CPU, requiring data centers to use around 40% fewer of them to support the same amount of GPUs. It has roughly double the performance per watt compared to Intel and AMD's chips. Arm expects to sell over $1 billion worth of AGI CPUs in the first year alone and hit $15 billion in annual revenue within five years, which would effectively quadruple the company's total revenues from the current $5 billion per year by 2031. This positions Arm to directly compete in Nvidia's market for the first time.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Arm's exact speed advantages depend on the actual workload, and there are plenty of cases where Nvidia still wins by large margins. Nvidia's CUDA platform has two decades of software, developer tools, and infrastructure that every AI team already relies on.

buy_recommendations.reason: Cerebras just completed the largest US semiconductor IPO ever and is directly competing with Nvidia in the data center AI accelerator market. Their wafer scale engine (WSE 3) is a fundamentally different architecture: instead of cutting wafers into individual chips, they turn the entire wafer into one massive chip with 62 times the size of competitors' chips, 19 times more transistors, and 44 times more AI cores compared to Nvidia's Blackwell. While having a quarter of the memory, the WSE 3 has 2600 times the memory bandwidth, allowing it to run Meta's Llama 4 Maverick model roughly 2.4 times faster than Nvidia's B200 because data moves across one giant chip instead of between multiple separate chips across cables and network switches. Cerebras is pairing this chip with Arm's AGI CPU, positioning them at the intersection of the major architectural innovations challenging Nvidia's dominance.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Cerebras's exact speed advantages depend on the actual workload, and there are plenty of cases where Nvidia still wins by large margins. Nvidia's CUDA platform has two decades of software, developer tools, and infrastructure that every AI team already relies on.

buy_recommendations.reason: Micron is the only US company making high-bandwidth memory for AI data centers, with competitors SK Hynix and Samsung based in South Korea and therefore more affected by tariffs, trade wars, and geopolitical conflicts. Micron's high-bandwidth memory is found in Nvidia's Hopper, Blackwell, and Rubin chips, AMD's Instinct MI300 and 400 series accelerators, and Google's TPUs. The company was already sold out of high-bandwidth memory for all of 2026 as of their earnings call. Micron reported record revenues of almost $24 billion last quarter, up nearly 200% year-over-year, with gross margins of 75% (better than most software companies), net income growing by almost 20x, and earnings per share growing by almost 30x. For next quarter guidance: 40% revenue growth, another 6% increase in gross margins, and 57% earnings growth quarter-over-quarter. Micron will make more money next quarter than any full year before 2025. The stock has skyrocketed 10x over the last year and tripled in price over the last 5 months but still trades at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of just 12, while other chip companies trade at 20 to 80 forward PE despite Micron's faster earnings growth.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Memory was historically a commodity, but Micron is now a core component of the AI revolution with exceptional growth.

buy_recommendations.reason: Rocket Lab is the only vertically integrated end-to-end space company that's publicly traded until SpaceX IPOs. While most space companies either build satellites or launch vehicles, Rocket Lab does all three. The Electron rocket is the most frequently launched small rocket in the world with a 3D-printed Rutherford engine (first rocket engine with 3D-printed combustion chamber, injectors, pumps, and propellant valves) that cuts manufacturing time from months to days and dramatically reduces cost per engine. Earlier this month Rocket Lab reported their best quarter ever with $200 million in revenue, up over 60% year-over-year, and a backlog that hit $2.2 billion, up 20% quarter-over-quarter and 100% year-over-year. The backlog has three buckets: small satellite launches on Electron and medium lift on Neutron, defense contracts for hypersonic test flights, and satellite/spacecraft manufacturing (2/3 of revenue). SpaceX is expected to IPO at roughly 60x forward revenue while Rocket Lab trades at 20x, suggesting Rocket Lab is relatively cheap even at an $84 billion market cap if SpaceX's valuation is justified. SpaceX is one of the first customers for Nvidia's Vera CPU, connecting the space and AI chip war narratives.
buy_recommendations.when_to_buy: The creator plans to wait for SpaceX's IPO (expected June 12th) and compare all space stocks side by side before making a final decision.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Space is the toughest market to operate in. Just days before this video, Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket exploded during a ground test in Florida, badly damaging the launch pad, causing the entire space sector to sell off. Competition in space lives and dies by launches. SpaceX is roughly 30 times bigger than Rocket Lab by revenue and launches 8 times more rockets per year, though they currently serve different market segments (big vs. small payloads).

key_warnings: Nvidia's reporting change to lump gaming GPU sales into an 'edge computing' segment that represents less than 8% of revenues gives competitors permission to directly compare their strongest business units to Nvidia's weakest and claim market share advantages, potentially inflating their stock prices as a result. Mainstream media focuses on headlines without looking below the surface, so stock prices are about to change in ways that reflect this competitive reality shift.

Nvidia is facing competition on multiple fronts: Qualcomm and Arm are shipping data center CPUs that compete with Nvidia's Vera CPUs; custom AI chips from Google, Amazon, and Cerebras are cheaper and more efficient for specific inference workloads; and companies like Apple, ARM, AMD, and Intel are competing in edge computing markets. While Nvidia won't outright lose these markets, competitors can now directly compare themselves to Nvidia in Nvidia's weaker markets.

The space sector is highly volatile and subject to catastrophic setbacks. Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket exploded during a ground test, causing the entire space sector to sell off the next day. Competition in space lives and dies by launches, making it an exceptionally risky sector.

cdpOpOntLeU.title: My Warning to SPCX Investors - Here's What You Need to Know

buy_recommendations.reason: Daniel Pronk chose to invest in Amazon as part of the infrastructure layer for artificial intelligence. He views Amazon as building the actual infrastructure for AI and functioning as a utility for AI rather than being a model company itself. This approach allows exposure to AI growth while avoiding the capital intensity and commoditization risks of AI model development.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: Daniel Pronk and Steve Eisman both argue that SpaceX is massively overvalued at a $2.5-3 trillion market cap based on speculative, momentum-driven hype rather than fundamentals. The business has become extremely capital intensive, with CapEx at 215% of revenue in Q1 (compared to 42% in fiscal 2023), largely due to AI/Grok investments that are not yet producing returns. The prospectus reads like science fiction with unrealistic projections (asteroid mining, space data centers) and the valuation assumes 50x revenue growth over 5 years to justify even conservative 20-30x sales multiples by 2030. The LLM business (Grok) appears to be commoditizing along with all other AI models, offering no differentiation or moats while consuming enormous capital. Steve Eisman expects Tesla could be merged into SpaceX, which would further dilute shareholder value since Tesla's business is also struggling (earnings declining for 4 years, EV margins compressing). The entire valuation is based on a $28.5 trillion TAM where 85% relates to AI, not actual space operations.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: Pronk explicitly states the stock is being held up by hype and air, with minimal fundamental basis. A major correction is likely when the 95% insider-locked shares begin unlocking starting August 11, releasing massive selling pressure over 6 months. Current share price movements are artificially inflated due to only 5% of float trading; with full float available, the stock could decline sharply as rational sellers enter the market.

stocks_to_avoid.reason: Daniel Pronk argues that Tesla is not a good business fundamentally. Earnings have been declining for the last 4 years, revenue has been flat for years, and the EV business is seeing declines in both gross profit and revenue. The core issue is that Tesla is being valued not as a car company (which is 95% of its actual business today) but as a highly profitable software, robotics, AI, and energy company combined. This means investors are already pricing in massive future product successes across multiple categories that may never materialize or may be far less profitable than expected. Steve Eisman emphasizes that the EV market is not a good business as China produces EVs much cheaper than Tesla. If SpaceX acquires Tesla (as Eisman expects), it would be a value-destructive move for SpaceX shareholders.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: If Tesla's future products (autonomous vehicles, energy, software) fail to deliver at the profitability levels currently priced in, the stock faces severe downside. Even if these products work, they may not be as profitable as current valuations assume, leading to significant multiple compression.

key_warnings: The entire tech sector, particularly AI-focused companies, is in a hype-driven momentum phase disconnected from fundamental valuations. Companies like Google, Meta, and Microsoft are raising massive amounts of capital for AI infrastructure despite already having strong cash flows, indicating the capital intensity of AI has become unsustainable and is transforming the sector from asset-light to asset-heavy. Pronk warns that LLMs are rapidly commoditizing with no differentiation or pricing power between ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, and others—despite hundreds of billions in CapEx spending, the products are increasingly interchangeable, suggesting future profitability is at risk.

There is a bifurcated market where speculative, hype-driven stocks like SpaceX are trading at extreme valuations (90+ times sales) while high-quality, fundamentally sound businesses are being ignored and trading below COVID crash valuations (e.g., Mastercard). This suggests significant long-term value exists outside the momentum names but investors are irrationally chasing hype.

SpaceX's IPO float mechanics create artificial price support that will evaporate: only 5% of shares are currently trading while 95% are insider-locked. Starting August 11 and continuing through December 9, massive share unlocks will release enormous selling pressure as long-term holders gain the ability to exit. This structural dynamic means the current price spike is not indicative of true demand and could reverse sharply when insiders can finally sell.

Retail investors are being lured into speculative positions by social media posts projecting unrealistic returns (e.g., $7,000 per share, $90-100 trillion market caps, 10x returns in 5 years) based on circular reasoning and science fiction scenarios. These projections assume 50x revenue growth in 5 years and ignore the capital intensity of the business. Pronk warns this is irrational market behavior, not rational investing, and recommends avoiding participation rather than shorting.

When it comes to the most advanced chips running AI training and inference at scale, TSMC holds something close to an absolute monopoly with its 3-nanometer and 2-nanometer process nodes being the cutting edge of what silicon can do today. AI has become the core of TSMC's business—the company's high-performance computing (AI accelerators, GPUs, and data center chips) increased 20% quarter over quarter to account for 61% of fourth quarter revenue. The company guided full-year 2026 revenue to grow above 30% in US dollar terms. TSMC is executing a coordinated multi-continent expansion of its 3 nanometer node with a new fab in Taiwan, a second Arizona fab, and Japan's second fab being upgraded to 3 nanometer. The CEO stated they are stepping up CapEx investments to increase N3 capacity, with the CFO noting that CapEx in the next three years will be significantly higher than the past three years (which totaled 101 billion), representing a capital investment cycle of a scale semiconductor manufacturing has never experienced. buy_recommendations.reason: ASML is a Dutch company that manufactures lithography systems, the machines that project circuit patterns onto silicon wafers to create semiconductors. Its extreme ultraviolet (EUV) machines are the only tools on Earth capable of printing today's most advanced chip designs, and no other company makes them. TSMC, Samsung, Intel, and every leading edge chip maker buy from ASML because there is no alternative. EUV is how 3 nanometer chips and 2 nanometer chips exist, and ASML's next generation high NA EUV platform is how the nodes beyond that will be printed. As chips become more complex, more EUV exposures are required per chip, and AI is accelerating this litho intensity even faster than any previous technology cycle. The CEO updated 2026 guidance, increasing the expected revenue range to between 36 billion and 40 billion euros while maintaining gross margin expectations of 51 to 53%. ASML is currently under-shipping demand and cannot build EUV machines fast enough to meet customer demand. By 2027, ASML aims to ship at least 80 tools (up from 44 in the prior year), effectively doubling the chip-making capacity it delivers through EUV alone. Additionally, AI-grade DRAM adoption is accelerating—DRAM now accounts for 51% of net sales systems (up from 30% in Q4 2025), representing a durable new dimension to ASML's growth driven by AI accelerators requiring high bandwidth memory. Once memory makers commit to EUV intensive nodes, they keep buying tools for years, making this a sticky revenue stream rather than a one-time event.
buy_recommendations.cautions: Primary risk is regulatory. ASML is already prohibited from selling its most advanced EUV chips to China, and the rules governing what it can sell and to whom are still evolving. China currently accounts for 20% of ASML's revenue, and any further tightening of restrictions or extensions of controls to additional product lines could trim that contribution further. Management acknowledged this directly, noting that 2026 revenue guidance range is set specifically to accommodate potential outcomes of ongoing discussions around export control. For a company with a hardware monopoly on the world's most critical technology, the risk isn't competition, it's policy, and the regulatory environment remains live and unpredictable.

While management does not anticipate near-term disruption, they acknowledged it was too early to quantify any potential impact on profitability for semiconductor manufacturers.

The company achieved 60+ billion in net deposits in 2024 with 35% annualized growth, and maintained 20% net deposit growth even during Q1 2025's difficult macro environment (government shutdown, software sell-off, global conflict). The addressable market is massive: $20 trillion in US retail brokerage, $20 trillion in US retirement, and $7-10 trillion in US advisory, with Robinhood at only $350 billion in assets under management. The company is gaining market share in options (recently #1), gaining in equities (#2 and compounding), and has new high-growth products (banking already at $2 billion deposits with 100,000+ customers; credit card at $10+ billion annualized GMV with 800,000 customers and millions on waitlist; prediction markets as fastest-growing product ever). The stock currently trades at a price-to-earnings ratio of 31, which is cheaper than any point in the entire AI revolution or the 3 years before ChatGPT launched. The global artificial intelligence market is expected to almost 19x in size over the next nine years, representing a compound annual growth rate of 38.5% through 2034. buy_recommendations.reason: Google owns its entire AI infrastructure stack: the chips (Tensor Processing Units), the data centers, the cloud, the Gemini models, and products and services used by billions of people worldwide. Google Cloud now generates over $80 billion in annual revenue with a backlog of almost half a trillion dollars, growing almost as fast as Amazon Web Services and Microsoft Azure combined. Google is the only company that runs more AI compute than any other company on Earth with their TPUs. Recently, Google started selling TPUs to outside customers, including a deal to sell up to a million chips to Anthropic, representing well over a gigawatt of new compute capacity coming online. The creator is so confident in Google's position that they note Google can arm their own rivals with their best chips and still win big due to their full-stack advantage.

The creator's investment thesis is that regardless of whether the market shifts towards Nvidia's GPUs, Google's TPUs, or custom chips made by Broadcom, they win because they hold all three companies. This hedging strategy protects against uncertainty in which chip architecture dominates the AI infrastructure market.

TSMC builds over 90% of all advanced chips on Earth and around 70% of the world's chips by revenue. They are the only company that can manufacture advanced chips for every side of the chip war at scale, making GPUs for Nvidia and AMD, smartphone processors for Apple and Samsung, and custom AI chips for Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. TSMC is so far ahead of every other foundry that they can simply set the price and customers have to pay because there is nowhere else for them to go. Regardless of whether Nvidia's GPUs, Google's TPUs, or Broadcom's custom chips win in the market, TSMC gets paid either way, making them a defensive position in the portfolio.

AI chips are only as fast as the memory that supports them. High bandwidth memory is one of the biggest bottlenecks of the entire AI buildout, and only three companies on Earth make it, with Micron being the only one based in the United States. Micron's memory is already pre-sold through the end of 2027, and the memory shortage is expected to get worse before it gets better as AI demand increases. AI companies are signing multi-year contracts and prepaying for chips that aren't even built yet. Unlike most vendors who sell one piece of the puzzle, Vertiv sells both power and cooling as a single end-to-end architecture from the grid to the chip, all optimized to work together. Companies like Schneider, Eaton, and Delta are racing to the same 800V standard, but only Vertiv and Schneider deliver the full power and cooling stack as a single system. Vertiv also builds direct-to-chip liquid cooling. Every gigawatt-scale AI factory being built right now needs what Vertiv is selling, with orders growing by 250% year-over-year, the fastest growth in Vertiv's history.

Before anyone can order Vertiv systems, they need grid-connected power to run them. Iron has over 4.5 gigawatts of secured power across Texas, Oklahoma, Canada, and Spain, all being used for AI data centers. Nvidia itself signed a 5-year, $3.4 billion cloud contract to run internal workloads on Iron's infrastructure. As part of that deal, Nvidia took a warrant to buy $30 million shares of Iron stock for $70 per share, which only pays off if Iron trades well above that price over the next few years. Microsoft also signed a 5-year, $9.7 billion deal for Iron's capacity and prepaid for 20% upfront. Iron's $3.4 billion annual recurring revenue target only uses about 10% of the power they already have secured, meaning they can support 10 times that amount once all their AI infrastructure is constructed and running.

He argues the stock was very expensive at peaks due to multiple expansion and has now corrected to fair value rather than becoming cheap. Netflix's business is maturing with decelerating growth rates: revenue growth expected to decelerate from 13.5% in Q2 2026 to 11.7% in 2027 and 10.3% in 2028. The streaming market is becoming increasingly competitive with YouTube and Amazon gaining market share—Netflix gained only 0.2% market share year-over-year while YouTube gained 1.7%, suggesting competitors are performing better. Pronk's DCF analysis yields only 13% compounded annual growth versus higher returns from competing stocks. The advertising business, while growing at 100%, remains a small portion of overall revenue. Relative to other market opportunities like Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, Brookfield, and Mercado Libre, Netflix does not offer the same attractive risk-reward profile.
stocks_to_avoid.risk: Pronk's DCF valuation suggests a fair value of approximately $79 per share with a future stock price around $105, implying limited upside from current levels and downside risk if growth decelerates faster than expected or competitive pressures intensify.

key_warnings: The streaming market is becoming significantly more competitive. Meta is launching Instagram on TV with Reels casting and horizontal video content to directly compete with Netflix and YouTube for TV watch time. YouTube has captured substantially more market share growth than Netflix year-over-year (1.7% gain versus Netflix's 0.2% gain), suggesting competitors are outperforming.

Netflix's growth rates are consistently decelerating and the business is maturing. Analyst expectations show revenue growth declining from current levels to 13.75% (full year), 11.7% (2027), and 10.3% (2028), indicating a structural slowdown in the business rather than a temporary issue.

Market valuation is not solely determined by distance from all-time highs. A stock down 45% from peaks does not automatically represent a buying opportunity if the stock was overvalued at those peaks. Netflix's decline was primarily driven by multiple contraction from an elevated valuation, and the stock has normalized to fair value rather than becoming a discount.
